quotes.verbatim_quote:
Extended chords use intervals outside of the root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th in their
construction.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational definition that distinguishes extended chords from basic triads and seventh chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because we don't have 10 fingers to play chords as a pianist would do, guitarists have
to leave some notes out when adding extended notes to their chord shapes.

Keep that in mind, and notice which notes are replaced as you add these fun chord
shapes to your harmonic vocabulary.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the core practical rule of guitar voicing — that guitarists must make deliberate omissions — and directs the student to observe those substitutions analytically.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Major 6 chords and major 6(9) chords, the first two types of extended chords in this
set, are different from the extended chords that follow because they don't contain a 7.

All the other extended chords (such as maj9), contain the 7th in their construction.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule draws a structural dividing line within the extended-chord family that governs how each chord type functions harmonically.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In a major 6 chord, the 6 replaces the 7:
quotes.why_load_bearing: This concise rule specifies the exact interval substitution that defines a major 6 chord and distinguishes it from a major 7 chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When a 6 and a 7 are both present in a major chord, it becomes a major 13 chord.
Major 13 chords contain both the 7 and the 6 (=13), as opposed to major 6 chords,
where the 7 is omitted.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines the precise interval logic that separates major 6 from major 13, a distinction critical to correct chord identification and usage.
